GitHub Pages Lets You Set Up Your Own 404 Page Too
Did you know GitHub Pages actually supports a custom 404 page out of the box? No extra config needed. This post walks through how to set one up.
Before getting to the setup, quick refresher on what a 404 page is.
If you've used the internet for any amount of time, you've hit one. Type a URL that doesn't exist, click a dead link — and a 404 page shows up.
Usually with copy like "Page not found", "Page not found — please check the URL", or just "404 Not Found".
What's the actual job of a 404 page? To tell the user one thing:
"You've landed on a page that doesn't exist — head back."

That sounds trivial, but UX-wise it matters. You don't know how a visitor got to your site — search engine, an external link, an internal link, whatever — and some of those links will be wrong. A 404 page tells them the page doesn't exist, and ideally points them somewhere useful.

It's also important for SEO. When a search engine crawler hits a missing page, the 404 response tells it "don't index this". Without that, you might end up with broken pages indexed under your domain, which drags your site quality scores down.
So is the 404 page important? Pretty much. A lot of sites customize theirs — better error copy, public service announcements, missing-children alerts — those are all common 404-page customizations.
Plenty of devs use GitHub Pages to host portfolios since most of us already have a GitHub account.
But when showing off your work, you'll sometimes hit a missing page, and GitHub Pages shows its default 404:
It's not bad. But if you care about polish, you'll probably want your own — a portfolio's 404 page is part of the impression.

If you'd rather write your 404 in Markdown, that works too — name it

Now you can write your 404 page content in Markdown.
When GitHub deploys your site, it looks for this file, and if it finds it, uses it as your 404 page. If not, it falls back to the default.
Pretty easy, right? Go customize your own 404 page.

GitHub Pages actually supports a custom 404 page out of the box — no extra config needed. This post explains what a 404 page is for (UX and SEO), what GitHub Pages' default 404 looks like, and how to drop a 404.html (or 404.md with permalink) into your repo root to ship your own.
